title: A Day as a DFAT Diplomat at an Australian Embassy
setting: Australian Embassy, Jakarta, Indonesia — Political Section
companySize: Embassy staff of ~120 (Australian-based and locally engaged), Political Section of 6 diplomats
description: Rebecca Tran is a Second Secretary (Political) at the Australian Embassy in Jakarta, three years into her career with the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade (DFAT). She joined through DFAT's graduate programme after completing a Bachelor of Arts/Laws at Melbourne University and spending a gap year teaching English in rural Indonesia, where she became fluent in Bahasa Indonesia. Her portfolio covers Indonesian domestic politics, democratic governance, and civil society. This is her first overseas posting — she spent her first two years in Canberra on the Indonesia desk in the South and Southeast Asia Division, which gave her strong headquarters context before going to post.

schedule.activity: Morning scan of Indonesian media and overnight cables
schedule.note: Reading Indonesian-language media before the day starts. A cabinet reshuffle was announced overnight — Rebecca needs to assess the implications for Australia's bilateral relationship. She also checks overnight cables from Canberra and other posts in the region.

schedule.activity: Drive to the Embassy compound in Kuningan, South Jakarta
schedule.note: Jakarta traffic is legendary. The 12 km commute can take 45 minutes to an hour. Rebecca uses the time to listen to Indonesian radio news and think about the day's priorities.

schedule.activity: Morning meeting with the Political Counsellor
schedule.note: The Political Counsellor (Rebecca's boss) briefs the section on priorities. The cabinet reshuffle is the main event. Canberra wants an assessment by close of business: who are the new ministers in portfolios relevant to Australia, what does the reshuffle signal about the President's priorities, and what are the implications for pending bilateral issues.

schedule.activity: Contact programme — phone calls to Indonesian interlocutors
schedule.note: Rebecca calls three contacts: a political journalist at Tempo who covered the reshuffle announcement, a staff member at the DPR (Indonesian parliament) who knows the new ministers' track records, and an academic at CSIS Indonesia who specialises in executive politics. Each conversation yields different pieces of the puzzle.

schedule.activity: Draft a diplomatic cable to Canberra
schedule.note: Writing a 3-page cable: SUBJECT: INDONESIA — CABINET RESHUFFLE: IMPLICATIONS FOR BILATERAL AGENDA. The cable follows DFAT's structured format: factual summary, analysis, assessment of implications, and recommended Australian response. Every assertion is sourced. The tone is precise and dispassionate.

schedule.activity: Cable review with Political Counsellor and Deputy Head of Mission
schedule.note: Two rounds of review. The Deputy Head of Mission strengthens a paragraph on trade implications and softens a characterisation of one minister that she considers premature. The cable is released to Canberra under the Ambassador's authority.

schedule.activity: Lunch with an Indonesian think tank researcher
schedule.note: A scheduled contact lunch at a restaurant near the Embassy. These relationships are the backbone of diplomatic reporting — built over months and years, they provide context, candour, and access that no open-source research can match.

schedule.activity: Consular briefing on an Australian citizen case
schedule.note: An Australian citizen has been arrested in Bali on drug charges. The Consular Section briefs the Political Section because the case may attract media attention and have political dimensions. Rebecca's role is limited — consular protection is handled by specialist staff — but she needs to be aware.

schedule.activity: Prepare talking points for the Ambassador's meeting
schedule.note: The Ambassador has a meeting tomorrow with the new Indonesian Foreign Minister. Rebecca drafts talking points on three bilateral issues: a pending maritime boundary negotiation, cooperation on counterterrorism, and a development assistance programme review.

schedule.activity: Attend a seminar at CSIS Indonesia on Indo-Pacific security
schedule.note: A panel discussion on ASEAN's response to South China Sea tensions. Attending events like this serves multiple purposes: learning, being seen (visibility matters in diplomacy), and expanding the contact network. Rebecca speaks with two new contacts afterward.

schedule.activity: Return to Embassy, update contact database and draft notes
schedule.note: Log the new contacts, write up key points from the seminar, and check whether Canberra has responded to this morning's cable. They have — the Indonesia desk wants additional detail on the new Trade Minister's stance on the Indonesia-Australia CEPA trade agreement.

schedule.activity: Representational event — National Day reception at another embassy
schedule.note: Attending a National Day reception at the French Embassy. Diplomatic social events are work — they are where relationships are built and maintained across the diplomatic corps. Rebecca speaks with counterparts from the US, Japanese, and Singaporean embassies about their read on the cabinet reshuffle.

responsibilities: Report on political, economic, and security developments in Indonesia through diplomatic cables to Canberra
Build and maintain a network of contacts across Indonesian government, parliament, media, academia, and civil society
Draft talking points, briefing papers, and policy advice for the Ambassador and visiting Australian ministers
Represent Australia at official events, seminars, and diplomatic functions
Support bilateral negotiations on trade, security, development, and consular matters
Monitor Indonesian media and public discourse for issues relevant to the bilateral relationship
Coordinate with other sections of the Embassy (trade, defence, aid, consular) on cross-cutting issues
Contribute to Canberra's whole-of-government policy development on Indonesia
challenges: Living and working in a foreign country for 3-4 years at a time — adapting to a new culture, climate, language environment, and social context every posting
The pace of diplomatic reporting — when a crisis hits, you may work 14-hour days for weeks, drafting cables, briefing ministers, and coordinating responses
Maintaining relationships with contacts who may have competing interests or who are under pressure from their own governments not to speak frankly
The gap between what you assess and what the government decides — your cable may recommend one course of action, but the political decision goes another way
Partner and family disruption — postings require relocating every 3-4 years, which is hard on partners' careers, children's schooling, and extended family relationships
Security constraints in some postings — restricted movement, threat of surveillance, and in hardship posts, genuine physical risk
rewards: Living and working in some of the most fascinating countries in the world, with deep cultural immersion that few other careers offer
Direct influence on Australia's foreign policy — your reporting and analysis shapes how the government understands and engages with other countries
Intellectual variety — one day you are analysing domestic politics, the next you are working on a trade negotiation, the next you are supporting a consular crisis
The privilege of representing your country — it carries weight, opens doors, and comes with a sense of purpose that is hard to replicate in other careers
A global professional network of highly capable people across governments, international organisations, and civil society
Strong benefits including housing allowance, hardship allowances for difficult postings, children's education support, and generous superannuation
careerPath: Third Secretary (graduate entry) → Second Secretary → First Secretary → Counsellor → Minister-Counsellor → Ambassador/High Commissioner → Deputy Secretary → Secretary of DFAT
Alternative: move to a multilateral posting (UN New York, WTO Geneva, OECD Paris) for a different style of diplomacy
Alternative: transfer to a related agency (ASIS, ASD, AUSTRADE) where diplomatic skills and regional expertise are valued
Alternative: leave the APS for international organisations (UN agencies, World Bank, Asian Development Bank) or the private sector (consulting, corporate affairs, risk advisory)
